news_summary: Efforts across Latin America and innovative AI research are advancing human rights and safety, while Glasgow prepares to host a more inclusive Commonwealth Games.
news_summary_fr: Les initiatives menées dans toute l'Amérique latine et les recherches innovantes en matière d'intelligence artificielle contribuent à faire progresser les droits de l'homme et la sécurité, tandis que Glasgow se prépare à accueillir des Jeux du Commonwealth plus inclusifs.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas que se están llevando a cabo en toda América Latina y la investigación innovadora en inteligencia artificial están contribuyendo a la promoción de los derechos humanos y la seguridad, mientras que Glasgow se prepara para acoger unos Juegos de la Commonwealth más inclusivos.

articles.title: From Mexico to Venezuela: A diaspora’s race to save lives
articles.summary: From Mexico, Venezuelans mobilized within hours, building trusted networks to deliver aid and proving that solidarity can cross any border.
articles.content:
When devastating earthquakes struck Venezuela, the response extended far beyond the country’s borders. Across Latin America and beyond, Venezuelans living abroad mobilized within hours, raising funds, organizing volunteers, and building trusted networks to deliver aid directly to survivors.

In Mexico, where decades of earthquake preparedness have fostered a powerful culture of civic solidarity, Venezuelan migrant, producer and anti-racist investigator Mariana Álvarez Castillo helped spearhead a grassroots relief effort alongside the communities of La Pitahaya Vegana, Masala CCS and Levantemos la Casa. Through a rapidly assembled network of volunteers, donors, and trusted local partners, they delivered urgently needed aid directly to those affected.

In this interview, Álvarez reflects on the huge efforts of the team, how the Venezuelan diaspora transformed grief into collective action, the challenges of coordinating humanitarian relief across borders, and what this remarkable mobilization reveals about the resilience, solidarity, and evolving identity of Venezuelans around the world.

Gabriela Mesones Rojo (GMR): What motivated you to organize so quickly after the earthquakes?

Mariana Álvarez Castillo (MÁC): Beyond the obvious shock and grief of knowing that so much irreversible damage had occurred in a matter of seconds, I think living in a seismic country like Mexico has taught us how critical the first hours after a disaster like this are. In the face of an event of this magnitude, the response has to be immediate. Here, the collective memory of the 1985 and 2017 earthquakes is still very much alive. These events are part of Mexico’s recent history, and they led to the development of protocols, early warning systems, drills, and emergency guidelines. Because of that, we quickly understood that the scale of the catastrophe in Venezuela would be devastating. We also know that our country, Venezuela, is not prepared to cope with earthquakes like these. Just a couple of hours after the quake, reports from La Guaira began to emerge, and by the following morning we were already making decisions about how to organize. That same day, we launched our initiative, and within seven hours we had delivered supplies, meals, and clean drinking water to four public hospitals thanks to the overwhelming response from donors. During that same week, we also established a collection center at La Pitahaya Vegana, made possible by an incredible network of volunteers.

GMR: What does this mobilization say about the Venezuela that exists beyond its borders?

MÁC: Venezuelan migrants are learning how to build community outside our country. Unlike countries such as Mexico, Puerto Rico, or China, whose migration histories span generations and have produced well-established communities abroad—like Mexicans in Chicago or California, or Puerto Ricans in New York—we don’t have a long-standing migrant tradition to draw upon. We’re creating that collective memory now. Migration on this scale is a recent phenomenon in Venezuela’s history, so it isn’t always easy to organize or know how best to respond. But in this case, different small groups quickly reached the same conclusion: we had to send money for whatever was most urgently needed — water, tools, medical supplies, medicines, and more recently, orthopedic fixation materials for survivors with severe injuries caused by collapsed buildings. There is also a deep sense of guilt that comes with being far away, and even with being safe. When you can’t be there to support your loved ones during such a horrific moment, the best response is action: finding a way to solve something, however small; contributing something, however insignificant it may seem.

GMR: What has been the biggest challenge in coordinating aid from Mexico — fundraising, logistics, or building trust?

MÁC: It’s important to say that experiencing the solidarity of the Mexican people has been deeply moving. They have supported us by donating, bringing supplies to collection centers, volunteering at fundraising events, helping facilitate the travel of the Topos rescue brigades, and offering words of encouragement and heartfelt embraces. We know that Venezuelan migrants in other countries haven’t always experienced the same level of empathy, and feeling alone and foreign during a tragedy like this can be heartbreaking. Perhaps the greatest challenge for us as migrants in Mexico is exactly what many people who lived through the earthquakes would have preferred: not being there. Walking through streets far from home, going to places where life appears to go on as usual. Going to work and functioning as though everything is normal. Finding the strength to focus on the present. Trying to pay attention to anything other than the relentless flow of news about the earthquakes. Essentially, pretending we’re okay.

GMR: Venezuela has a long history of distrust regarding humanitarian aid. How are you ensuring transparency?

MÁC: The three of us are friends who work on academic and culinary projects. Throughout this process we’ve stood behind the initiative with our personal identities and social media accounts, but also through Antroposabores, Conuco, and La Pitahaya Vegana — trusted community-based projects that have spent years building strong, deeply committed networks. From the beginning, we also partnered with the Caracas restaurant Masala CCS, which was preparing to open its doors to the public when the earthquakes struck. Instead, they chose to donate their facilities, staff, and kitchen to prepare meals for doctors, rescue workers, and survivors. Through their volunteers, we’ve built a network in Caracas that allows resources to reach civilians directly, without intermediaries or bureaucracy. Donations go straight to a trusted member of our network in Caracas, who purchases whatever is needed and delivers it directly to the places where it’s required, avoiding customs procedures or checkpoints that could delay or divert aid. We also follow a strict accountability policy: every donation is recorded, every purchase receipt is kept, and photos, videos, and testimonials are shared on our social media. This allows every donor to see exactly how their contribution was used and the direct impact it has had. More recently, we launched ‘Levantemos la Casa’ (‘Let's Rebuild the Home’), a series of events organized by the Venezuelan community in Mexico City, where artists, poets, producers, designers, filmmakers, and entrepreneurs donate their work to raise funds. Every dollar goes directly toward supporting those affected by the earthquakes through cultural gatherings that strengthen community while providing relief.

GMR: Is there anything about Mexico’s model of civic organization that you’d like to see replicated in Venezuela?
articles.reasoning: The article describes a rapid, grassroots humanitarian response by the Venezuelan diaspora and Mexican communities to devastating earthquakes in Venezuela. It highlights cross-border solidarity, effective aid delivery, transparency, and community resilience, all of which have meaningful positive impact on affected populations and demonstrate significant social mobilization and humanitarian action.
articles.personality_title: Venezuelan diaspora and Mexican communities unite quickly to aid earthquake survivors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Venezuela was hit by powerful earthquakes that caused serious damage and suffering. Many Venezuelans live outside their country, including in Mexico, where people have experience responding to earthquakes.

**What happened** – Soon after the earthquakes, Venezuelans living in Mexico organized fast to help. They raised money, gathered supplies, and worked with local groups to send food, water, and medical aid directly to hospitals and survivors in Venezuela. This effort began within hours and included volunteers, donors, and trusted partners.

**Impact** – This quick, organized response shows how Venezuelans abroad are building strong communities and helping each other despite being far from home. The aid reached survivors without delays, thanks to trusted networks and clear records of donations. Mexican solidarity also played a big role, with many locals supporting the effort through donations and volunteer work.

**What's next step** – The group plans to continue raising funds through cultural events like art shows and performances. They aim to keep helping survivors rebuild their homes and lives. This effort may also inspire more organized responses from migrants in other countries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Venezuelans living abroad and their Mexican allies quickly formed trusted networks to deliver emergency aid, showing new strength in community and cooperation across borders.
articles.personality_title_fr: La diaspora vénézuélienne et les communautés mexicaines s’unissent rapidement pour aider les survivants du tremblement de terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Venezuela a été frappé par de puissants tremblements de terre qui ont causé de graves dégâts et souffrances. De nombreux Vénézuéliens vivent à l’étranger, notamment au Mexique, où les habitants ont l’habitude de répondre aux catastrophes sismiques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Peu après les tremblements de terre, les Vénézuéliens vivant au Mexique se sont rapidement organisés pour aider. Ils ont collecté des fonds, rassemblé des fournitures et travaillé avec des groupes locaux pour envoyer nourriture, eau et aides médicales directement aux hôpitaux et aux survivants au Venezuela. Cette action a commencé en quelques heures et a réuni bénévoles, donateurs et partenaires de confiance.

**Impact** – Cette réponse rapide et organisée montre comment les Vénézuéliens à l’étranger construisent de solides communautés et s’entraident malgré la distance. L’aide est arrivée sans retard grâce à des réseaux fiables et à une gestion claire des dons. La solidarité mexicaine a aussi joué un grand rôle, avec de nombreux habitants locaux qui ont soutenu l’effort par des dons et du bénévolat.

**Prochaine étape** – Le groupe prévoit de continuer à collecter des fonds par des événements culturels comme des expositions d’art et des spectacles. Ils souhaitent continuer à aider les survivants à reconstruire leurs maisons et leurs vies. Cette initiative pourrait aussi inspirer d’autres réponses organisées chez les migrants dans d’autres pays.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les Vénézuéliens vivant à l’étranger et leurs alliés mexicains ont rapidement créé des réseaux fiables pour apporter une aide d’urgence, montrant une nouvelle force communautaire et une coopération au-delà des frontières.
articles.personality_title_es: La diáspora venezolana y las comunidades mexicanas se unen rápidamente para ayudar a los sobrevivientes del terremoto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Venezuela fue golpeada por poderosos terremotos que causaron graves daños y sufrimiento. Muchos venezolanos viven fuera de su país, incluyendo en México, donde la gente tiene experiencia en responder a terremotos.

**Qué pasó** – Poco después de los terremotos, los venezolanos que viven en México se organizaron rápidamente para ayudar. Reunieron dinero, juntaron suministros y trabajaron con grupos locales para enviar comida, agua y ayuda médica directamente a hospitales y sobrevivientes en Venezuela. Este esfuerzo comenzó en pocas horas e incluyó voluntarios, donantes y socios de confianza.

**Impacto** – Esta respuesta rápida y organizada muestra cómo los venezolanos en el extranjero están formando comunidades fuertes y ayudándose mutuamente a pesar de la distancia. La ayuda llegó sin demoras gracias a redes confiables y registros claros de las donaciones. La solidaridad mexicana también jugó un papel importante, con muchos locales apoyando con donaciones y trabajo voluntario.

**Próximo paso** – El grupo planea seguir recaudando fondos mediante eventos culturales como exposiciones de arte y presentaciones. Quieren continuar ayudando a los sobrevivientes a reconstruir sus hogares y sus vidas. Este esfuerzo podría inspirar respuestas organizadas similares en migrantes de otros países.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los venezolanos que viven en el extranjero y sus aliados mexicanos formaron rápidamente redes confiables para entregar ayuda de emergencia, mostrando una nueva fuerza comunitaria y cooperación entre países.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a network of intertwined hands made of vibrant threads stretching from a stylized map of Mexico to a map of Venezuela, with symbolic elements like carefully packed aid boxes, flowing water, and glowing lanterns representing hope and solidarity, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: How an AI research tool helped challenge support for police violence in Brazil
articles.summary: In 2023, Brazil recorded 45,747 intentional violent deaths — among the highest rates in the world. Like many democracies, the country has long grappled with the issue of large-scale public support for state violence.
articles.content:
This article, written by Daniel Stanley, Marianna Araujo and Diogo Santos, is published here through an agreement between Instituto Fogo Cruzado and Future Narratives Lab with Global Voices. For more information, see the report “Challenging narratives on police violence in favelas.”

In 2025, as Instituto Fogo Cruzado, Brazil’s leading civil society monitor of armed violence, and UK-based research organisation Future Narratives Lab were finalising a study on messaging around police violence in favelas, we were shaken by a grim turn of events. In a single favela raid in Rio de Janeiro on October 28, 122 people were killed by Brazilian police, the deadliest policing operation in the country’s history. Polls conducted in the days after the raid showed continued broad public support for such action, despite longstanding evidence that the raids have no impact on reducing crime rates.

In 2023, Brazil recorded 45,747 intentional violent deaths — among the highest rates in the world. Like many democracies, the country has long grappled with the issue of large-scale public support for state violence, which has contributed to a broader societal sense of fear, fatalism, and desensitisation. Changing this requires understanding what works to challenge certain misconceptions. The broad support for the October 28 raids, despite the casualties, highlighted the critical need for grassroots organisations to discover messaging that resonates with the public, especially in a polarised political environment like Brazil.

Understanding what drives support for violence

Civil society organisations working on contentious issues face a problem: many audiences are supportive of policies and actions that run counter to their own interests, yet are difficult and expensive to reach for research, especially in politically uncertain or highly polarised contexts. Such organisations, particularly in the Global South, often lack the funding and resources to carry out the types of in-depth research that Global North-based civil society organisations use. Focus groups and qualitative interviews are the standard route to understanding why people hold the views they do, but they are slow and costly, often putting them out of reach for the smaller organisations doing the most urgent work.

What if an artificial intelligence (AI) tool could lower these barriers and allow small, grassroots organisations to understand how their target audiences think? The large-scale adoption of AI tools for everyday use has created greater opportunities to learn research skills and process information. But, as with any technological innovation, there are ethical considerations around the use of such technology, including the implications of using tools created by Big Tech corporations and the environmental consequences of data centres.

Clearly, it is not a technology that can be used lightly. As we developed the tool, we had many discussions about how to use AI in a way that was consistent with our overall beliefs and aims. Ultimately, we decided to maintain an approach we saw as capable of contributing to advancing a human rights movement, while taking specific steps to protect user privacy, prioritise open-source software-based solutions, and plan future actions to address further concerns.

Using AI to uncover underlying beliefs

Before this project, Future Narratives Lab had developed an AI Interviewer tool specifically for civil society organisations looking to understand their audiences. After a successful trial of the tool in a project testing messages on the criminal justice system in the UK, Instituto Fogo Cruzado approached Future Narratives Lab for support using it in a new project on state-sponsored violence in Brazil, funded by the International Resource for Impact and Storytelling (IRIS), as part of their “New Tech, New Rules” programme.

The main use of the tool in this project was for primary research, laying the foundations for the development of new message and counter-narrative approaches. Twenty-eight in-depth qualitative interviews were conducted via the chatbot-based AI interviewer, with each participant able to engage in the conversation using their own devices via a supplied URL and participant code.

Participants were selected and recruited based on a demographic profile identified in prior desk research as being more likely to support an increase in police violence as a means to reduce crime: those who identified as Christian (Evangelical or Roman Catholic), had not completed higher education, and had a household income of less than 500 BRL (approximately USD 98).

The AI interviewer carried out a customised, structured but adaptive conversation with each participant for up to 30 minutes, asking follow-up questions and probing for inconsistencies and reasoning. Each interview was based on an overall brief setting out what the research aimed to achieve and what happened, concurrently, but unique to each participant. To give a sense of how a typical interview went:

It consistently started with the question: “What comes to mind when you hear ‘violence’ in Brazil?” Participants responded with a range of associations and, sometimes, specific experiences.

The interviewer would then ask responsive questions to clarify or request further explanation as needed. Other subjects were then explored, such as who the main victims of such violence are and who holds responsibility for reducing it.

One key point was the presentation of official data showing a decrease in homicides, to which participants were asked to react. Many expressed surprise or disbelief; these responses were then probed for further explanation.

Questions then explored different, narrower aspects of the topic, including opinions on police violence specifically, the root causes of crime, and recommended actions to address it.

The process of message development that followed uncovered a range of new insights and potential opportunities for persuasion.

What the AI Interviews allow

The ability to carry out multiple interviews, concurrently and remotely, enables organisations to compress what would normally take weeks of fieldwork into days at a fraction of the cost. For under-resourced civil society organisations across the world, this tool creates a significant shift: it makes in-depth audience research about what would move their audiences viable.

In this case, Fogo Cruzado used the tool to investigate what drives underlying beliefs and opinions about police violence. The AI interviews, combined with their years of experience and expertise, suggested three potential areas where messaging could sway reluctant audiences:

police unpreparedness undermines the effectiveness of raids;

raids leading to innocent killings via stray bullets;

the failure of raids to address root causes.

This foundational research directly led to the next step in the study: surveying over 500 Brazilians from a “hard to persuade” demographic in an RCT-style test, to understand which of these messaging options might contribute to a decrease in support for police raids.

What we found

The message that performed best was not centred on victims, empathy, or structural injustice — the framing narrative that advocates in the sector often reach for first. It was a pragmatic critique of police competence: “The police aren’t sufficiently trained or prepared, so carrying out raids in crowded favelas only leads to more shootouts and more innocent victims.” This framing emerged directly from the AI-conducted interviews, where participants repeatedly described police as underprepared rather than malicious.

Equally striking, Evangelical participants, who in Brazilian political discourse are often assumed to be aligned with conservatism, especially in a context where churches frequently collaborate with security forces, were less supportive of police raids than other groups. Their open-text responses revealed moral frameworks rooted in redemption, structural critique, and a distrust of elites, which sits in tension with the punitive politics often attributed to Evangelicals.

The big picture

Favela residents are routinely misrepresented in both domestic and international political and media narratives. Our study raises their voices in ways that aren’t often done. It makes us rethink which audiences we consider “persuadable.”

The affordable, fast, and transferable AI methodology was a key factor in how we were able to make this happen, and our goal is for other grassroots organisations to do the same. To start, Fogo Cruzado has already shared information on this approach with 20 Brazilian organisations through a “communication camp,” ahead of a crucial general election in October 2026, in which public security will be a central issue.

As the impact of AI on society becomes an ever more heated topic of discussion, there are increased expectations and responsibilities to ensure that its use takes into account the many ethical considerations at play.

Between our organisations, we feel in a strong position to do this — Future Narratives Lab has published about the negative impacts of mainstream AI use, and Fogo Cruzado has a close connection with grassroots communities to ensure their views are taken into account.

Steps have already been taken to move the AI interviewer away from more extractive “frontier” models, like Claude or ChatGPT, toward models that respect privacy and data protection and uphold higher overall standards. In a time when we’re seeing populism rise globally, the possibility of changing minds seems far-fetched, but with technology allowing us to meet audiences where they are, we get closer to building collective power.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the innovative use of AI tools by grassroots civil society organizations in Brazil to better understand and challenge public support for police violence, a major human rights issue. This approach enables under-resourced groups to conduct in-depth research quickly and affordably, leading to more effective messaging that could reduce support for harmful policing practices. The story has broad societal significance, clear substance, and positive real-world impact on advancing human rights and public discourse in a highly polarized context.
articles.personality_title: AI helps Brazilian groups better understand and challenge support for police violence
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Brazil faces a serious problem with violence, recording over 45,000 intentional violent deaths in 2023. Many people in the country support police actions that often cause harm, especially in poor neighborhoods called favelas. Changing public opinion on this issue is difficult but important.

**What happened** – In 2025, two groups—Instituto Fogo Cruzado from Brazil and Future Narratives Lab from the UK—used a new artificial intelligence (AI) tool to better understand why some Brazilians support police violence. They conducted 28 detailed interviews using an AI chatbot with people likely to support harsher police actions. This tool allowed them to gather information quickly and cheaply, even though these groups usually don’t have much money for research.

**Impact** – The research showed that people were less convinced by messages about victims or injustice. Instead, they responded best to messages pointing out that police are often not well trained, causing more harm during raids. The study also found that some groups, like Evangelicals, were less supportive of police violence than expected. This new understanding helps activists create better messages to change minds and reduce support for violent policing.

**What's next step** – Using these findings, the researchers surveyed over 500 Brazilians to test which messages work best to change opinions. Instituto Fogo Cruzado also shared this AI research method with 20 other Brazilian groups. This work will continue ahead of Brazil’s 2026 elections, where public safety is a key topic.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using AI interviews, Brazilian civil groups discovered new ways to challenge support for police violence by highlighting police unpreparedness, enabling more effective efforts to change public opinion.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA aide les groupes brésiliens à mieux comprendre et contester le soutien à la violence policière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Brésil fait face à un grave problème de violence, avec plus de 45 000 morts violentes intentionnelles en 2023. Beaucoup de personnes soutiennent les actions policières qui causent souvent des dégâts, surtout dans les quartiers pauvres appelés favelas. Changer l’opinion publique sur ce sujet est difficile mais nécessaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, deux groupes—Instituto Fogo Cruzado du Brésil et Future Narratives Lab du Royaume-Uni—ont utilisé un nouvel outil d’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour mieux comprendre pourquoi certains Brésiliens soutiennent la violence policière. Ils ont mené 28 entretiens détaillés grâce à un chatbot IA avec des personnes susceptibles de soutenir des actions policières plus dures. Cet outil leur a permis de recueillir des informations rapidement et à moindre coût, alors que ces groupes ont souvent peu de moyens pour la recherche.

**Impact** – La recherche a montré que les gens étaient moins convaincus par les messages axés sur les victimes ou l’injustice. Ils répondaient mieux aux messages soulignant que la police est souvent mal formée, ce qui cause plus de dégâts lors des opérations. L’étude a aussi révélé que certains groupes, comme les évangéliques, étaient moins favorables à la violence policière que prévu. Cette nouvelle compréhension aide les militants à créer de meilleurs messages pour changer les opinions et réduire le soutien à la violence policière.

**Prochaines étapes** – En se basant sur ces résultats, les chercheurs ont sondé plus de 500 Brésiliens pour tester quels messages fonctionnent le mieux pour changer les opinions. L’Instituto Fogo Cruzado a aussi partagé cette méthode de recherche IA avec 20 autres groupes brésiliens. Ce travail va continuer avant les élections de 2026 au Brésil, où la sécurité publique est un sujet clé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce à des entretiens menés par IA, des groupes civiques brésiliens ont trouvé de nouvelles façons de contester le soutien à la violence policière en soulignant le manque de préparation de la police, rendant les efforts de changement d’opinion plus efficaces.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA ayuda a grupos brasileños a entender y desafiar el apoyo a la violencia policial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Brasil enfrenta un grave problema de violencia, con más de 45,000 muertes violentas intencionales en 2023. Muchas personas apoyan acciones policiales que a menudo causan daño, especialmente en barrios pobres llamados favelas. Cambiar la opinión pública sobre este tema es difícil pero necesario.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, dos grupos—Instituto Fogo Cruzado de Brasil y Future Narratives Lab del Reino Unido—usaron una nueva herramienta de inteligencia artificial (IA) para entender mejor por qué algunos brasileños apoyan la violencia policial. Realizaron 28 entrevistas detalladas con un chatbot de IA a personas que probablemente apoyan acciones policiales más duras. Esta herramienta les permitió recolectar información rápida y económicamente, aunque estos grupos normalmente tienen pocos recursos para investigaciones.

**Impacto** – La investigación mostró que las personas respondían menos a mensajes sobre víctimas o injusticia. En cambio, respondieron mejor a mensajes que señalaban que la policía a menudo no está bien preparada, causando más daño en las redadas. El estudio también encontró que algunos grupos, como los evangélicos, apoyaban menos la violencia policial de lo esperado. Este nuevo entendimiento ayuda a activistas a crear mejores mensajes para cambiar opiniones y reducir el apoyo a la violencia policial.

**Próximos pasos** – Usando estos hallazgos, los investigadores encuestaron a más de 500 brasileños para probar qué mensajes funcionan mejor para cambiar opiniones. Instituto Fogo Cruzado también compartió este método de investigación con IA con 20 otros grupos brasileños. Este trabajo continuará antes de las elecciones de 2026, donde la seguridad pública será un tema clave.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Usando entrevistas por IA, grupos civiles brasileños encontraron nuevas formas de desafiar el apoyo a la violencia policial destacando la falta de preparación policial, permitiendo esfuerzos más efectivos para cambiar la opinión pública.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing a stylized, abstract favela landscape with interconnected speech bubbles shaped like softly glowing lanterns floating above, each containing symbolic icons: a shield with a crack representing police unpreparedness, a dove flying free symbolizing hope and peace, and an open hand releasing chains to signify breaking cycles of violence and misunderstanding.

articles.title: Glasgow set to welcome the world for scaled back Commonwealth Games
articles.summary: Athletes from 74 countries and territories will compete for 215 gold medals over 10 days of competition.
articles.content:
When the fastest man on the planet won Commonwealth relay gold for Jamaica in 2014, more than 50,000 fans cheered him home at Hampden.

But the athletes seeking to emulate Usain Bolt when the Games return to Glasgow - including mile world record holder Josh Kerr - will be competing at Scotstoun Stadium in front of 11,000 spectators.

This time round almost everything is on a smaller scale but organisers are confident fans will create a Tartan Army-style atmosphere for the 3,000 competitors who will be chasing glory.

From Friday until 2 August, 215 gold medals will be up for grabs - compared to 261 when Scotland last staged the Games - but Glasgow 2026 will see a record 47 para-sports events.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the upcoming Commonwealth Games in Glasgow with a record number of para-sports events, promoting inclusivity and athletic achievement on a global scale. Despite a scaled-back event, it maintains significant positive impact by fostering international sportsmanship and increased visibility for para-athletes, benefiting a broad audience.
articles.personality_title: Glasgow to host smaller but inclusive Commonwealth Games with record para-sports events
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Commonwealth Games is a big sports event where athletes from many countries compete in various sports. Glasgow hosted the Games before in 2014, attracting huge crowds.

**What happened** – From July 19 to August 2, 2026, Glasgow will host the Commonwealth Games again. This time, the event will be smaller with fewer spectators—about 11,000 at Scotstoun Stadium instead of over 50,000. Around 3,000 athletes from 74 countries will compete for 215 gold medals.

**Impact** – Even though the Games are smaller, they will include a record 47 para-sports events, which are competitions for athletes with disabilities. This is the most ever at the Commonwealth Games, showing a strong focus on inclusion and giving more para-athletes a chance to shine on a big stage.

**What's next step** – Organizers hope fans will bring energy and excitement despite the smaller crowds, creating a lively atmosphere for all athletes. The success of these Games could encourage even more support and growth for para-sports in future international events.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Glasgow’s 2026 Commonwealth Games will be smaller but more inclusive, setting a record for para-sports and welcoming thousands of athletes from around the world.
articles.personality_title_fr: Glasgow accueillera des Jeux du Commonwealth plus petits mais inclusifs avec un record d’épreuves para-sportives
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Jeux du Commonwealth sont un grand événement sportif où des athlètes de nombreux pays s’affrontent dans différents sports. Glasgow a déjà organisé ces Jeux en 2014, attirant un très grand public.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Du 19 juillet au 2 août 2026, Glasgow accueillera de nouveau les Jeux du Commonwealth. Cette fois, l’événement sera plus petit avec moins de spectateurs – environ 11 000 au stade Scotstoun contre plus de 50 000 auparavant. Environ 3 000 athlètes de 74 pays se disputeront 215 médailles d’or.

**Impact** – Même si les Jeux sont plus petits, ils comprendront un record de 47 épreuves para-sportives, des compétitions pour les athlètes en situation de handicap. C’est la première fois qu’il y en a autant, ce qui montre un fort engagement pour l’inclusion et donne plus de chances aux para-athlètes de briller.

**Prochaine étape** – Les organisateurs espèrent que les fans apporteront énergie et enthousiasme malgré la taille réduite des foules, créant une ambiance vivante pour tous les athlètes. Le succès de ces Jeux pourrait encourager un plus grand soutien et développement des para-sports lors des événements internationaux futurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les Jeux du Commonwealth de Glasgow 2026 seront plus petits mais plus inclusifs, établissant un record pour les para-sports et accueillant des milliers d’athlètes du monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Glasgow albergará unos Juegos de la Commonwealth más pequeños pero inclusivos con récord de eventos para para-deportistas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Juegos de la Commonwealth son un gran evento deportivo donde atletas de muchos países compiten en diferentes deportes. Glasgow organizó estos Juegos en 2014, con una gran cantidad de espectadores.

**Qué pasó** – Del 19 de julio al 2 de agosto de 2026, Glasgow volverá a ser sede de los Juegos de la Commonwealth. Esta vez, el evento será más pequeño con menos espectadores, unos 11,000 en el estadio Scotstoun en lugar de más de 50,000. Cerca de 3,000 atletas de 74 países competirán por 215 medallas de oro.

**Impacto** – Aunque los Juegos serán más pequeños, incluirán un récord de 47 eventos para para-deportistas, que son competiciones para atletas con discapacidades. Esto muestra un fuerte enfoque en la inclusión y da más oportunidades a estos atletas para destacar en un gran escenario.

**Próximo paso** – Los organizadores esperan que los fans aporten energía y emoción a pesar de las multitudes más pequeñas, creando un ambiente animado para todos los atletas. El éxito de estos Juegos podría impulsar más apoyo y crecimiento para los para-deportes en eventos internacionales futuros.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los Juegos de la Commonwealth de Glasgow 2026 serán más pequeños pero más inclusivos, estableciendo un récord para los para-deportes y dando la bienvenida a miles de atletas de todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a stylized stadium filled with a smaller, close-knit crowd draped in tartan patterns, surrounding a glowing golden relay baton passing between abstract, dynamic silhouettes symbolizing athletes, with subtle hints of para-sport equipment integrated into the scene, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones.
